title: Immunological signature of chronic spontaneous urticaria (Review)
quotes: The majority of tryptase-rich mast cells are predominantly located in mucous membranes (T-mast cells), while those containing tryptase, carboxypeptidase and chymase predominate in the connective tissue of the skin (TC-mast cells)
Mast cells in patients with urticaria, similar to those in healthy individuals, are located in the superficial and deep dermis, preferentially in the perivascular and periaxonal spaces
summary: Dobrican et al. (2022) review mast cell biology in skin: skin mast cells are predominantly MCTC (connective tissue type) containing tryptase, chymase and carboxypeptidase, located perivascularly in superficial and deep dermis.

title: Dynamically upregulated mast cell CPA3 patterns in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
quotes: mast cells may act in a pro-inflammatory manner by releasing histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins, and cytokines. Another important mechanism by which mast cells influence their tissue surroundings is through their specific proteases.

tryptase and chymase have been the most studied and are also used for the classical division of mast cells into "connective tissue-like" mast cells (MC TC cells; containing tryptase and chymase) and "mucosal-like" mast cells (MC T; lacking chymase)
Contrasting the lung and gut, skin mast cells had a very high CPA3-staining intensity, a feature also seen among the few MCTs present in skin
summary: Siddhuraj et al. demonstrate that skin mast cells are predominantly MCTC with uniquely high CPA3 expression. Mast cell proteases (tryptase, chymase, CPA3) are key effectors in tissue homeostasis and inflammation.
